sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage: A lab studies standing waves on a string of fixed length L = 1.2 m with both ends fixed. Students drive the string at resonant frequencies and record the first three harmonics: 50 Hz, 100 Hz, and 150 Hz. The wave speed is constant because string tension and linear density do not change. The group uses fn = nv/(2L) to connect harmonics with wave speed.
questions.stem: What is the fundamental frequency of the string?

explanations.D: Correct. The fundamental is the first harmonic, n = 1. Figure 1 shows n = 1 at 50 Hz.
explanations.C: 25 Hz is not shown as a resonant frequency for this string.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. First three resonant frequencies of a string fixed at both ends.

questions.stem: What is the wave speed on the string?

explanations.C: Correct. For the fundamental, f1 = v/(2L). Thus v = 2Lf1 = 2(1.2 m)(50 Hz) = 120 m/s.
explanations.B: 100 m/s does not follow from the given length and frequency.
explanations.D: 240 m/s incorrectly doubles the correct result.

questions.stem: If string tension were increased, what would happen to resonant frequencies?

choices.D: Only the third harmonic would disappear

explanations.B: Correct. Wave speed on a string increases with the square root of tension. Since fn is proportional to v, increasing tension raises all harmonic frequencies.
explanations.A: Higher tension raises rather than lowers wave speed.
explanations.C: Changing tension changes wave speed and therefore frequencies.
explanations.D: The harmonic series remains; it shifts to higher frequencies.

questions.stem: How many antinodes are present in the third harmonic of a fixed string?

explanations.A: Correct. For a string fixed at both ends, the nth harmonic has n antinodes. The third harmonic has three antinodes.
explanations.C: Two antinodes describe the second harmonic.
explanations.D: Four antinodes describe the fourth harmonic.

questions.stem: Why are only certain frequencies resonant?

choices.D: Boundary conditions require nodes at both fixed ends
choices.A: The string can only vibrate if frequency is below 50 Hz
choices.B: Energy is not conserved at nonresonant frequencies
choices.C: The wavelength must always equal the string length exactly

explanations.D: Correct. Fixed ends must be nodes. Only wavelengths that fit an integer number of half-wavelengths along the string satisfy that boundary condition, giving discrete resonant frequencies.
explanations.A: Frequencies above 50 Hz can resonate as higher harmonics.
explanations.B: Energy conservation is not the reason resonance frequencies are discrete.
explanations.C: The fundamental has λ = 2L; higher harmonics have shorter wavelengths.

questions.passage: A simplified Doppler ultrasound model measures blood moving toward a probe. The transmitted ultrasound frequency is 2.0 MHz, and measured frequency shifts increase with flow velocity. At velocities 0.20, 0.40, and 0.60 m/s, the shifts are 520, 1040, and 1560 Hz. Students use the proportionality between Doppler shift and flow velocity for a fixed probe angle and sound speed in tissue.
questions.stem: What relationship does Figure 2 show between frequency shift and velocity?

explanations.C: Correct. The plotted points form an approximately straight line through the origin. Doubling velocity from 0.20 to 0.40 m/s doubles the frequency shift from 520 to 1040 Hz.
explanations.A: An inverse relationship would make the shift decrease as velocity increases.
explanations.B: The graph clearly shows a systematic relationship.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Doppler frequency shift measured for blood moving toward an ultrasound probe.

questions.stem: What shift would be expected at 0.50 m/s?

explanations.B: Correct. The slope is about 2600 Hz per m/s. At 0.50 m/s, Δf ≈ 2600 × 0.50 = 1300 Hz.

questions.stem: For blood moving away from the probe, the reflected frequency would most likely be what?

choices.D: Zero because waves cannot reflect from blood

explanations.A: Correct. Motion away from the probe produces a negative Doppler shift, so the reflected frequency is lower than the transmitted frequency.
explanations.B: Motion toward the probe gives a higher reflected frequency; away gives lower.
explanations.C: Motion changes the observed frequency in a Doppler measurement.
explanations.D: Ultrasound can reflect from moving blood cells.

questions.stem: Why is ultrasound useful for measuring flow in this context?

choices.D: Frequency shifts provide information about motion along the beam direction
choices.A: The sound wavelength becomes infinite in blood

explanations.D: Correct. The Doppler effect relates frequency shift to relative motion. Measuring the shift allows estimation of velocity components along the ultrasound beam.
explanations.A: Ultrasound does not require infinite wavelength.
explanations.B: The described measurement is motion-based, not only thermal.
explanations.C: The probe is not described as altering blood viscosity.

questions.stem: Which factor would need to remain fixed for the simple proportionality in Figure 2?

choices.C: The angle between the ultrasound beam and flow direction
choices.D: The chemical identity of hemoglobin only

explanations.C: Correct. Doppler shift depends on the cosine of the beam-flow angle. If the angle changes, the same flow speed can produce a different measured shift.
explanations.A: Patient height is not a direct variable in the Doppler shift equation.
explanations.B: Display color does not affect the physical frequency shift.
explanations.D: Hemoglobin identity is not the parameter controlling the proportionality in this model.
